environment.hardware: No special hardware required. Verified on Apple Silicon (aarch64) macOS 26.5.2 (Darwin 25.5.0), no GPU/cluster/large-memory dependency: the Kitaev-chain sweep uses momentum grids of at most 2000 points and the cluster-state stabilizer engine operates on an 8-10 qubit chain (a handful of short bit-vectors and Gaussian elimination over F2), both trivial in-memory computations; the LaTeX build is a standard single-machine pdfTeX+BibTeX compile.
environment.software: GHC 9.10.1 (installed via ghcup, reachable at ~/.ghcup/bin/ghc-9.10.1; I chose this version for consistency with the with-compiler pin used by this project's sibling Haskell packages, since bordism-realizability itself declares no compiler pin of its own) with cabal-install 3.16.1.0 for the Haskell suite, built via a scratch .cabal/cabal.project I authored outside the repository under my job tmp directory (see concerns). The ambient default toolchain on PATH was GHC 9.14.1 (Homebrew) with the same cabal-install 3.16.1.0. pdfTeX 3.141592653-2.6-1.40.29 (TeX Live 2026) plus BibTeX 0.99e (TeX Live 2026) for the paper build.
dependencies: base >=4 && <5 (bundled with GHC; this bound was chosen by me for the scratch build file I had to write -- it is not declared anywhere in the repository for this package, since no .cabal file for bordism-realizability exists)

QuickCheck (required by Properties.hs's `import Test.QuickCheck`; no version bound is declared anywhere in the repository for this package; it resolved from the local cabal package store for ghc-9.10.1 with no visible network fetch during my build, apparently already populated from building a sibling package in this same environment)

concerns: Unlike its sibling packages in this repository (src/lattice-eft-equivalence/ ships lattice-eft-equivalence.cabal + cabal.project; src/positivity-cstar-norms/ ships positivity-cstar-norms.cabal), src/bordism-realizability/ ships zero build metadata: no .cabal file, no cabal.project, no Makefile. I confirmed this by listing the directory (only Kitaev.hs, Stabilizer.hs, Properties.hs, Main.hs are present) and cross-checking against the two sibling packages that do carry cabal files. A reader who clones the repo and runs `cd src/bordism-realizability && cabal build` gets an immediate 'no cabal file found' failure, not a compile. To build and run the suite for this review I wrote my own scratch bordism-realizability.cabal/cabal.project under my job tmp directory, with hs-source-dirs pointing back at the real source directory via a relative path (cabal rejects absolute paths in hs-source-dirs, so I had to compute the path '../../../../../Documents/Development/topological-phases-of-matter/src/bordism-realizability' rather than use the absolute path directly, which cabal's parser rejected on the first attempt). I did not add, move, or edit any file inside src/bordism-realizability/ itself -- the real source directory is untouched. Once given any package description the four modules built and ran with no issues (see below), so this is a packaging omission rather than a code or math defect, but it is a concrete reproducibility gap: nothing in the repository as committed lets a fresh clone build this specific package.

The paper's Section 'Computational verification' (sec:haskell) names the directory (src/bordism-realizability/) and describes what each module does, but gives no build/run command (no `cabal build`, `cabal run`, or `runghc` instruction) and no statement of the GHC or dependency versions used to produce the numbers quoted in the text (e.g. 'for |mu|<2 the winding is 1 and M=-1'). Combined with the missing .cabal file, a reader has to reverse-engineer the four-module dependency graph (Main imports Kitaev, Stabilizer, and Properties; Properties imports Test.QuickCheck; Kitaev and Stabilizer import nothing beyond Prelude) before they can compile anything at all.

Because no cabal file ships with the package, there is also no version-bound or freeze-file record anywhere in the repository for this package's one external dependency (QuickCheck). The bounds I used in my scratch build file (base >=4 && <5, QuickCheck unbounded) are my own invention for the purpose of this review, modeled on the sibling packages' convention -- they are not anything the repository actually specifies for bordism-realizability, so I cannot report on 'the project's declared bounds' here because none exist. QuickCheck resolved from the local cabal store with no visible network fetch, so I cannot independently confirm which QuickCheck version a from-scratch clone on a different machine would resolve to, though the API surface used (Arbitrary, Gen, choose, suchThat, forAll, quickCheckResult) is long-stable and unlikely to break across recent major versions.

As with the sibling packages in this repository, the ambient default toolchain on PATH is GHC 9.14.1 (Homebrew, cabal-install 3.16.1.0), while GHC 9.10.1 (the version I selected, for consistency with the project's established convention, since this package declares no compiler pin of its own) is installed only via ghcup and is not on PATH by default; invoking cabal without first adding ghcup's bin directory to PATH would silently resolve to the ambient 9.14.1 instead. I did not test whether the code also builds warning-free under 9.14.1 -- I used 9.10.1 throughout. Cabal also prints a cosmetic 'Unknown/unsupported ghc version detected' warning for 9.10.1 under this cabal-install release; this does not affect build or test outcomes.

No external repository URL, DOI, or Zenodo-style archive appears anywhere in the manuscript or bibliography for the accompanying code; the only pointer is the in-repo relative path 'src/bordism-realizability/' given in Section 6. All four source files do carry an explicit MIT license header, which is why code_availability is recorded as open_source rather than unspecified, but a reader holding only the PDF, without the shared project checkout, has no way to fetch the code.

data_availability is recorded as unspecified because this paper's computational content is a closed-form/finite verification (a discretized winding-number sweep and an exact finite stabilizer-group computation), not an empirical dataset; 'data availability' does not meaningfully apply to its content, and no dataset claim appears anywhere in the text.
